generalities.text: Main THE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF PURE MATERIA MEDICA By TIMOTHY F. ALLEN, A.M., M.D. Presented by Médi-T TARAXACUM. Taraxacum Dens-leonis, Desf. (Leontodon Taraxacum, Linn.) Natural order , Compositæ. Common name , Dandelion; (G.) Löwenzahn. Preparation , Tincture of the whole plant just before the perfection of the flower. Authorities. ( 1 to 5 , from Hahnemann.) 1 , Franz; 2 , Gutmann; 3 , Kummer; 4 , Langhammer; 5 , Rasazemsky; 6 , George Smyth, M.D., Lancet, 1845 (2), p. 506, a lady, believing herself troubled with a liver complaint, took a dessertspoonful of the extract twice a day for rather more than a week. MIND. ► Talkativeness and incessant prattling, [4] . ► Religious, pious mood, happy, contented with himself and his condition, [4] . ► Very much inclined to laugh, [4] . ► Without occupation he is quite gloomy; does not know what to do, and cannot make up his mind to do anything, [1] . ► Ill-humored, in the morning, and disinclined to any occupation or to talking (after twenty-five hours), [4] . ► Irresolution and dread of work, though it progresses well as soon as he undertakes it, [1] . URINARY ORGANS. ► *Frequent desire to urinate, with discharge of much urine (after three hours); frequent desire, with scanty discharge (after twenty-five hours), [4] . [It may be seen, from this symptom, the first part of which represents the primary action, the second the secondary or persistent reaction of the organism, how perverted is the customary practice which attempts to cure with Taraxacum chronic swelling associated with diminished secretion of urine, If its nature is at first to increase the quantity of urine, its persistent reaction is to diminish it all the more. On the contrary, it is of use, homopathically, in forms of diuresis (diabetes) when the other symptoms correspond to Taraxacum, when the disease is not of miasmatic origin, as often happens. -Hahnemann.] ► Pressure to urinate, without pain (after one hour), [4] . ► Urine high-colored, and considerably diminished in quantity, [6] . GENERALITIES. [230.] ► After three days she began to feel rather uncomfortable and restless in herself; a sort of fidgetiness, as she expressed it, and general uneasiness, for which she could not account, as she had often been much worse indisposed without experiencing such miserable feelings, [6] . ► An inner feeling, as though he were very sick; all the limbs are painful when touched, or when in an uncomfortable position, [1] . ► Feeling of weakness over the whole body; loss of strength, so that he constantly wished to lie or sit, when he was in a condition of semi-consciousness, as on falling asleep, [1] . ► Nearly all the symptoms appear only while sitting, they almost all disappear while walking, [2] . SKIN. ► Objective. ► After eight days, on waking in the morning, she discovered the upper part of her body covered with a rash, to which her attention was first directed in consequence of its incessant tingling and itching; the itching, in short, was so urgent, that at first she could not possibly refrain from scratching, which afforded much satisfaction and relief for the time; but, as the relief proved only temporary whilst the operation seemed greatly to increase rather than diminish the irritation, by producing a greater abundance of the eruption and increased redness of the skin, she was, therefore, afraid to indulge in it. When I saw her there was no eruption on the face, but it was slightly swollen, and exhibited merely patches of efflorescence or redness, without any thickening or elevation of the cuticle. On the forearm and hands the eruption, when examined, appeared to be of a mixed character, lichen and urticaria combined; the lichenose or papular eruption (the papula about the size of a pin's head, and of a purple or dark-red color) was dispersed over the whole of the arms, back, and front, but thicker about the wrists and bindings of the elbows than anywhere else, in which place it was also clustered together in patches upon an inflamed base. The heads of numerous papulæ appeared to have been broken off, and afterwards covered with a brownish incrustation, as if blood had first exuded and then become hardened; but this no doubt was occasioned partly by friction of the dress, and partly by the scratching to which these parts were, in the first instance, subjected, for the purpose of allaying the distressing tingling and itching, from which there is scarcely any respite in such cases. The urticaria occupied principally the front of the arms; the wheals or elevations were not very numerous or prominent, unless friction was had recourse to, by which they could be produced almost to any extent; they were stated to come out and go in again, two or three times in the course of the day, and to be always very large and abundant in the morning; the chest and around the waist were also said to be thickly covered, and the redness intense, [6] . ► A pimple, on the right side of the scalp, above the temple, painful on touch, as though the place were suppurating (after fifteen hours), [4] . ► A pimple in the middle of the hair of the left eyebrow, with pressing pain when touched (after twenty-seven hours), [4] . ► A suppurating pimple, on the upper part of the left cheek, with red areola, with gnawing pain on touch (after twenty-four hours), [4] . ► Suppurating pimple on the right wing of the nose (after eight hours), [4] . ► Papular eruptions on the hands, especially on the sides of the fingers, also on the back of the hand, with some itching, [3] . [270.] ► Immediately on falling asleep he began, to perspire all over, so that he was frequently awakened thereby, when he always had heat over the whole body, much more distinct glowing heat in the cheeks; became quite wet from the perspiration, but was vigorous in the morning, [4] . ► On falling asleep in the evening, in bed, immediately perspiration all over, which continued through the whole night; in the morning he was active, [4] . ► Slight perspiration over the whole body, (after twenty-two hours), [4] . (after twenty-three hours), [4] . ► Much perspiration between the toes, especially of the right foot, [3] . CONDITIONS. ► Aggravation. ► ( Morning ), On awaking, eyes agglutinated. ► ( After drinking ), Empty eructations. ► ( After eating ), Chilliness. ► ( Expiration ), Pressure sternum; pressure from within against ribs. ► ( Holding head erect ), Heaviness in occiput. ► ( Inspiration ), Stitches in chest; stitch beneath left ribs. ► ( While lying ), Sticking pain in spine. ► ( Rubbing ), Stitch in upper arm. ► ( While sitting ), Pressive pain in forehead; pain in left temple; stitches in left temple; stitches on inner side of thigh; tearing in hollow of knee; pain in right sole. ► ( Stooping ), Pressure in pit of stomach. ► ( While standing ), Pain in frontal bone; pressure in small of back; pain in left leg; sticking in right leg; stitches on outer side of left leg; pain on back of foot; pulling from little toe up tibia. ► ( Walking ), Pain in occiput; tearing in occiput; pain in left groin; sticking in right side of chest; pain in right breast; stitches in thigh; sticking on right great toe. ► ( Walking in open air ), Vertigo; crawling in forehead; stitch in left temporal region; sneezing; shaking over whole body. ► Amelioration. ► ( Standing ), Stitch in left temporal region. ► ( Stooping ), Heaviness in occiput. ► ( While walking ), Nearly all symptoms. Sensation as if the larynx were pressed together. Pulsative throbbing and jerking in shoulders and arms. Jerkings of muscles of arms. Twitching in muscles of l. forearm. Shootings in arms and elbows. Drawings and tearings in forearm and wrist. Eruptions of pimples on hands and fingers. Icy coldness of ends of fingers. Pressive pain in three last fingers of r. hand. Shootings in thighs, knees, calves, soles, and toes. Stitching pain in l. thigh. Pressive pain in l. calf. Jerking pain in r. calf, ceasing quickly when touched. Drawing pain in dorsum of r. foot; when standing; stitches when sitting. Severe or fine stitching pains in r. sole. Burning sensation in knees, legs and toes. Profuse perspiration between toes. This remedy acts well in all affections of the liver; sour taste; darting pains in outer parts. Affections in l. upper side; r. lower side; back of foot; sole of foot. While lying down; from fat food; while resting. > From moving; when walking. Painful tenderness of all limbs, esp. when touched, and when they are in a false position. The limbs are Movable, but it feels as if the power to move them were impeded. Sensation of weakness and uneasiness throughout body, with constant necessity to lie down or to sit. Almost all symptoms appear while the patient is seated, and are dissipated by walking.

Vertigo on walking in the open air, as if intoxicated, the head fell at one time to the left, at another to the right side (aft. 2 1/4h.). When walking in the open air unsteady step and vertigo, as though he would fall forwards (aft. 10 h.). When walking in the open air great confusion and dizziness of the head; he seemed to stagger as if giddy. Sometimes contraction and whirling above the nose in the forehead, like vertigo, sometimes sensation as though the brain were distended here and there, painless. Sensation in the head as if the brain were constricted by a soft pressure from all sides. When walking in the open air aching crawling pain in the forehead, which spreads out from the centre of it, as though there were something alive in it (aft. 4 h.). A sensation in the head compounded of pressure and itching. Pressure deep down in the occiput and heaviness there (aft. 9 1/2 h.). Heaviness of the head and redness of the face. In the sinciput pressive pain out towards the forehead. Pressive stupefying pain on the forehead, as after a debauch (aft. 1 h.). Aching pain in the right temple (aft. 35 h.). Burning pressive pain in the head going upwards. Pressive pain in the head from within outwards (aft. 2 1/2 h.). Heaviness in the occiput which goes off every time he stoops, and returns on raising up and keeping the head straight, and then it is worst. When sitting aching stupefying pain in the whole forehead, so that he became unconscious when reading and did not know where he was, accompanied by nausea; he felt better only in the open air (aft. 1 3/4 h.). (Lr.]. Constant pressive pain on the forehead (aft. 4 h.). Drawing aching pain in the temple. When standing drawing pressive pain on the frontal bone. (f-h1]. When sitting drawing pain at the left temple, which ceases when walking and standing (aft. 5 h.). When walking tearing pain in the occiput, which goes off when standing still. When walking tearing on the occiput outwardly. Shooting tearing in the occiput behind the right ear. Tearing stitches in rapid succession on the left side of the forehead. (. A persistent shooting pain in the left side of the head, for six hours. When sitting needle-pricks in the left temple, which go off when standing (aft. 1 1/2 h.). When walking in the open air a violent persistent stitch in the left temporal region, which went off when standing (aft. 38 h.). Sharp stitches externally on the left side of the forehead, which are not allayed by touching (aft. 13 h.). Obtuse shooting pressure on the forehead (aft. 1/2 h.). [f£h1]. A pimple on the hairy scalp on the right side above the temple, which is painful when touched, as if the part were festering (aft. 15 h.). (f-h1]. The anterior skin of the hairy scalp is tense, as though it were tightly fastened to the crown. A pimple in the centre of the hairs of the left eyebrow with pressive pain when touched (aft. 27 h.). Contracted pupils (aft. 4 h.). Dilated pupils (aft. 26 h.). In the morning on waking the eyelids are stuck together with matter, for several days. A kind of inflammation of the eyes; the eyes are intolerant of light and are constantly watering, with a pressure on the right upper eyelid, as if something were there which he in vain endeavoured to wipe away. Eye-gum, more in the morning than during the day. Burning in the left eyeball (aft. 11 1/2 h.). Violent burning in the right eyeball towards the inner canthus. Fine burning prickling in both left eyelids (aft. 1/2 h.). Shooting burning pain in the left eyeball (aft. 20 h.). A burning shooting in the left eyeball, towards the outer canthus (aft. 1/2 h.). Sharp shooting pain in the right eye. Acute aching, as from a grain of sand in the right inner canthus, with sensation as if the eyelids were swollen there. (In the evening hardness of hearing; something seems to be before the ears; he only hears in a dull manner.). Inward pressure in the interior of the left ear. Chirping in the left ear, as from grasshoppers (aft. 33 h.). In the meatus auditorius externus tearing, and behind the ramus of the lower jaw sharp pressure. Stitches behind the ear, with tearing down the side of the neck. In the right ear a shooting from within outwards, which always receded inwards again. Itching burning shooting in the right ear. Drawing pain in the auricle (aft. 5 h.). A sharp pressure in the right cheek (aft. 1/2 h.). An aching stitch in the cheek. A suppurating pimple on the upper part of the left cheek, with red areola, which when touched has a gnawing pain (aft. 24 h.). A suppurating pimple on the angle of the right ala nasi (aft. 8 h.). Two attacks of epistaxis from the left nostril, at noon before eating (aft. 30h.). The upper lip cracks in the middle (aft. 6 h.). A suppurating pimple at the right commissure of the mouth (aft. 49 h.). Sudden itching below the chin (aft. 1 h.). In the lower part of the parotid gland and on the cervical muscles, and from the sternum to the mastoid process, an acute pain on moving the jaw and neck. Twitching on the side of the neck, interiorly (aft. 15 h.). (f-h1]. Sharp boring stitches in the left side of the neck from within outwards, for some minutes (immediately). Pressive twitching in the muscles of the nape behind the left ear (aft. 3 1/2 h.). Pricking in the left side of the nape, as from a rather blunt needle, when standing, which went off on sitting down (aft. 1 1/2 h.). Pressive shooting in the nape. Pressive pain like blows on two incisor teeth, more in their crowns. Blood flows from the carious teeth on the right side (which tastes sour). ({Kr.]. On chewing food sensation in the teeth as if they were set on edge by sour fruit (aft. 37 h.). Drawing pain in the carious teeth of the right side, which extends up the cheek to the superciliary arch. Burning shooting in the left side of the tongue (aft. 9 h.). White furred tongue (aft. 2 3/4 h.). White fur on the tongue which gradually exfoliates in patches (aft. 11 1/2 h.). The tongue is covered with a white skin, with raw feeling in it, it then exfoliates in patches and leaves dark red, tender, very sensitive places (aft. 34h.). In the morning on waking a quite dry brown furred tongue. Collection of saliva in the mouth and sensation as if the larynx were compressed (aft. 31 h.). Difficult deglutition; a kind of pressure as from an internal swelling in the throat. Sharp pressure on the anterior wall of the esophagus and larynx when not swallowing, which excites cough, but goes off when swallowing. Water of a sourish taste collects in the mouth. The mucus hawked up tastes quite sour and sets the teeth on edge (aft. 3, 4h.). Dryness and shooting in the throat. Dry sensation in the fauces and a bitter mucus there, which makes the voice hoarse. On the tip of the tongue butter tastes repulsively salty-sour; on the palate, however, it tastes as usual. Butcher's meat, but especially the gravy of roast meat, tastes quite sour when it comes in contact with the tip of his tongue. Before eating bitter taste in the mouth; but food has a natural taste. A bitter taste rises up the esophagus into the mouth. Tobacco-smoke has no taste, it causes burning in the throat, almost like heartburn, and takes away the breath; this is removed by drinking. Bitter eructation and hiccup. Empty eructation, which lasted several days, and came on especially after drinking (aft. 1/2 h.). Nausea, accompanied by anxiety, when sitting, which goes off when standing (aft. 2 1/2 h.). Nausea, as from overloading with fat food; he thought he must vomit, with aching stupefying pain in the forehead-he only got better in the open air (aft. 1 3/4h.). Qualmishness and nausea in the esophagus (aft. 2 3/4 h.). (f-h1]. After eating great chilliness, and especially after drinking. Painless movement and rumbling in the abdomen. Rumbling in the umbilical region, towards the left side. A quickly occurring, persistent movement in the hypogastrium as if bubbles were developed in it which burst (aft. 5 1/2 h.). Tension in the scrobiculus cordis and pressure on the ensiform cartilage when stooping. Aching under the ribs on the left side. Aching pain in the left side of the abdomen. Pinching in the belly (aft. 1 1/4 h.). Bellyache : pinching in the abdomen followed by discharge of flatus (aft. 3, 16 h.). Persistent aching stitches in the left side of the abdomen (aft. 24, 30 h.). Boring pain from within outwards, in the umbilical region, towards the right side. Tensive aching stitches in the right side of the abdomen. (f-h1]. Single, violent and sharp stitches, sometimes in the left side of the upper abdomen, sometimes in the left or right side of the abdomen, sometimes also in the hypogastrium (aft. 14, 31 h.). A severe stitch in the side of the abdomen, lasting a minute. Burning stitches in the left hypogastrium towards the genitals (aft. 25 h.). Pressure in the left lumbar region from within outwards. Itching pricking pain in the right abdominal muscles. Painless gurgling in the muscles of the left hypogastrium. Feeling of pain in the left groin when walking, as if dislocated, which is somewhat diminished by standing and touching (aft. 6 h.). Tickling itching on the perineum, between the anus and pudendum, which compels scratching (aft. 14 h.). [f:h1]. Voluptuous itching on the perineum, which compels scratching, which caused an eroding pain in this part, lasting many hours (aft. 32 h.). The first day a second, not hard stool, evacuated with difficulty and with much pressing (aft. 8 1/2 h.). The first day a third, less hard stool, evacuated with much pressing (aft. 16 h.). Stool sooner than usual and pappy; but the urging to evacuate continued without anything more passing. Urging to urinate without pains (aft. 1 h.). Frequent urging to urinate with copious discharge of urine (aft. 3 h.). (*From these two symptoms, (SS 118 and 119) the first of which is primary action, but the second secondary action, or permanent reaction of the organism, we see how perverse is the ordinary practice, when it is attempted to cure chronic dropsical effusions with diminished urinary secretion by means of dandelion. If, as it is its nature to do, it at first produces a greater discharge of urine, it diminishes the secretion all the more in its permanent secondary action. On the other hand, it would be more likely to effect homeopathic service in certain kinds of morbid diuresis (diabetes) for which dandelion is otherwise suitable, provided no miasmatic cachexy is the cause of the affection-as, indeed, is often the case.). Frequent urging to urinate with scanty discharge of urine (aft. 25 h.). ({Lr.]. Tickling on the prepuce, which compels rubbing (aft. 7 1/2 h.). A constant boring pain in the glans penis. A fine stitch in the left testicle. Burning shooting in the right testicle. Seminal emission on alternate nights. Long-continued, involuntary erections (aft. 9 d.). When walking in the open air frequent sneezing (aft. 4, 28 h.). Tickling irritation to short cough in the region of the pit of the throat, the coming on of which he is sensible of several seconds previously, but is unable to suppress it (aft. 40 h.). A boring and digging pain in the right side of the chest, more severe and persistent when walking (aft. 3 h.). Pressure in the left side of the chest under the axilla. In the right side of the chest, from the hepatic region up into the chest, on a space larger than a hand, a pressure from within on the ribs during expiration, when standing. Burning aching in the sternum, more severe during expiration than inspiration (aft. 1/2 h.). An aching stitch in the right side of the chest, which went off on inspiring and expiring more strongly; but when he pressed on the spot it returned more violently and extended farther, as a prolonged stitch-pain (aft. 2h.). A stitch in the right side of the chest (aft. 4 h.). Obtuse shooting in the left side of the chest (aft. 1 h.). In the left side of the chest shooting towards the back. (f-h1]. When standing stitches darting inwards in the chest during inspiration. (Fz.]. When walking continued shooting in the right side of the chest. Violent shooting in the sternum (aft. 6 h.). Stitch in the right side of the chest, just below the axilla (aft. 1 1/2 h.). Under the last rib of the left side, towards the back, three violent stitches, one at every breath. A severe stitch in the region of the sixth rib. Violent shooting in the left side of the chest, at the lowest rib. Shooting in the right side of the diaphragm, when lying on that side. On the shoulder end of the right clavicle a boring shooting drawing. Twitching in the left intercostal muscles. Twitching in the right intercostal muscles (aft. 14 h.). On taking a full inspiration tensive pain in the region of the diaphragm (aft. 11 h.). Aching in the sacrum. Gentle pressure in the sacrum when standing. Painless running in the sacrum. Tensive shooting in the back towards the right side. Aching shooting pain in the whole spine towards the right side, when lying, with difficult respiration, especially severe in the sacrum. A persistent obtuse stitch in the right scapula, from within outwards (aft. 21 h.). Vibration and quivering in the right scapula. Pulsating throbbing on the left shoulder, for a minute. Twitching in the top of the left shoulder. Painless vibration on the left shoulder, with chilliness all over. Twitching in the muscles of the left upper arm, on the outer side (aft. 4 h.). Quivering in the upper arm. In the interior of the upper arm pulsating throbbing, in fits. Pressive pain in the muscles of the left upper arm (aft. 30 h.). Pressive pain on the inner side of the left arm. Pain like electric shocks on the outer side of the left upper arm. Shooting pain on the inner side of the left upper arm. Sharp shooting on the outer side of the left arm. At the back of the upper arm a series of painful, sometimes violent, needle-pricks, which are removed by rubbing. Intermittent stitches on the outer side between the elbow and middle of the right upper arm, when at rest. Shooting in the right elbow-joint. Pricking in the left forearm, when at rest and when moving (aft. 13 h.). {Lr.]. Sharp stitches in the right forearm, which go off on touching (aft. 13 h.). Twitching in the muscles of the left forearm (aft. 10 h.). Burning in the right forearm. Pressive pain on the inner side of the right forearm. (f-h1]. In the forearm frequently recurring drawing pains. In the left wrist-joint a tearing drawing, which spreads into the three last fingers. On the hands a papular eruption, especially on the sides of the fingers, also on the back of the hand, with some itching. Burning pain in the third and fourth fingers of the left hand. Shooting pain in the fourth finger of the left hand. Aching pain in the three last fingers of the right hand. The tips of the fingers are icy cold (aft. 6 h.). Twitching in the muscles of the left natis extending downwards. Itching in the muscles of the left natis. Quivering in the upper muscles of the thigh (aft. 2 h.). Shooting pain in the whole of the left thigh (aft. 9 1/2, 10 1/2 h.). When sitting boring shooting on the inner side of the thigh. Quite at the top of the thigh in front a part is painful as if bruised, worse when touched than when walking. Pressure on the inner side of the right thigh, when sitting and standing, but not when walking (aft. 2 h.). Eroding itching on the left thigh, compelling scratching (aft. 7 1/2 h.). {Lr.]. Tearing in the hough, on its outer tendon, when sitting. (f-h1]. Drawing shooting pain on the outer side of the right knee, when at rest and when moving (aft. 3 h.). A shooting pain pervading the whole knee-joint. On the outer side of the knee sharp pressure when he flexes the leg. Burning pain in the left patella. A persistent burning pain anteriorly in the right knee. (f-h1]. Burning pain on the outer side of the right leg. Burning pain on the lower part of the right leg. Burning anteriorly on the tibia. In the right leg a violent pricking, going from below upwards, when standing, which went off when sitting (aft. 30 h.). On the outer side of the left leg needle-pricks darting upwards, when standing, which go off when sitting (aft. 31 h.). Drawing pains in the legs when sitting and when walking. Weakness of the legs, especially when going upstairs. The right leg is when walking weaker than the left, and yet its muscles feel more tense. Eroding gnawing pain on the right leg (when standing) (aft. 1 1/2 h.). {Lr.]. Tearing pain on the outer border of the left leg (when standing) (aft. 2 h.). Throbbing, obtuse stitches in the right calf, just below the hough. Shooting in the right calf. Constant burning stitches in the calf (when standing). Aching pain in the left calf (aft. 3/4 h.). Twitching pain in the right calf, which goes off quickly when touched (aft. 1 h.). Violent itching on the left calf on lying down in the evening, which compelled scratching, but continued to itch after scratching; the part then became red and exuded (aft. 17 h.). A stitch in the dorsum of the left foot, towards the big toe (aft. 37 h.). A stitch in the right inner ankle when sitting (aft. 32 h.). Itching pricking on the inner ankle. When standing a drawing pain on the dorsum of the right foot, which went off when sitting (aft. 1 h.). When standing an aching drawing pain in the dorsum of the left foot (aft. 3/4 h.). In the left ankle-joint a tearing drawing. Burning pain on the outer side of the right foot. Burning drawing on the dorsum of the left foot (aft. 37 h.). Pressure on the dorsum of the right foot, when sitting (aft. 22 h.). Itching on the dorsum of the right foot, which was removed by scratching (aft. 1 3/4 h.). [fth1]. On the dorsum of the foot some vesicles which itched. (f-h1]. Stitches from the dorsum of the foot into the sole (aft. 1 1/2 h.). Boring pain in the right sole. Burning boring in the left sole towards the little toe. Sometimes violent, sometimes fine shooting pain in the right sole, from within outwards, when sitting (aft. 10, 21 h.). Itching pricking in the right sole (aft. 32 h.). Burning pressive pain in the right sole towards the toes when sitting. A tug, going from the little toe up the tibia, when sitting. (f-h1]. Attacks of burning in the toes, especially on the top of the right big toe. Shooting in the right big toe towards the second toe. Burning shooting in the right big toe when walking (aft. 9 h.). Tearing stitches in the toes. Violent itching on the fourth toe of both feet (aft. 25 h.). Much sweat between the toes, especially of the right foot. (f-h1]. All the limbs move easily, but he feels as if the moving power were in a constrained state. Weak feeling in the whole body; a languishing state of all the powers, so that he was always disposed to lie or sit down, when he felt in an intermediate state between consciousness and unconsciousness, as when going to sleep. Inward feeling as if he were very ill; all the limbs were painful when touched and in a wrong position. Almost all the sufferings come on only when sitting; they almost all go off when walking. When sitting frequent yawning, as though he had not slept enough (aft. 5 1/2 h.). Sleepiness by day (when reading); the eyes even closed so that he must actually lie down; the sleepiness went off when moving (aft. 5 h.). Unconquerable drowsiness after a meal; on awaking he had urging to urinate with some burning, but only before and after the discharge of urine. On listening to scientific subject he goes to sleep in spite of all efforts to keep awake, and immediately vivid dream visions occur. Anxious, vivid, unremembered dreams. Vivid unremembered dreams. At night dreams full of quarrelling. Voluptuous dreams. Frequent waking from sleep with much tossing about in bed, he could find rest nowhere. Frequent waking from sleep as if he had slept enough. On waking from sleep, slight perspiration all over the body, which causes smarting all over the skin, compelling him to scratch (aft. 23 h.). On going to sleep in bed in the evening he immediately perspired all over the body, and this he did all night; in the morning he felt lively. Immediately he fell asleep he began to perspire all over, so that he often woke up in consequence, when he felt each time a heat all over the body but a much greater glowing heat in the cheeks; he got quite wet from perspiration, but was lively in the morning. Slight sweat all over the body (aft. 22 h.). When walking in the open air sudden warmth of the face, as also of the rest of the body, without thirst (aft. 37 h.). Sensation of heat and heat in the face, with redness (aft. 1 1/2 h.). The face hands, and the rest of the body are hot, without thirst (aft. 6 1/2h.). A chilliness throughout the body (aft. 26 h.). Chilliness or some hours with persistent aching pain in the head. When walking in the open air violent rigor all over the body, like an attack of fever, without thirst or heat thereafter (aft. 2 1/2 h.). Irresolution and disinclination for work, though it goes on very well as soon as he makes a commencement. Without occupation he is quite dull; he cannot compose himself, and yet cannot make up his mind to do anything. In the morning dissatisfied and disinclined for business or conversation (aft. 25 h.). Very much inclined to laugh. Loquacity and constant chattering. Religious, cheerful disposition, happiness, contented with himself and his position. Thuya occidentalis (Arbor vitae)

Exhausted, inclined to sit or lie down ; semi-conscious. Debility, loss of appetite, profuse, exhausting night sweat ; thirst ; restless sleep. Left : drawing or stitches in temples ; stitches in forehead ; burning sticking in eyeball ; nosebleed ; aching from neck into ear ; epididymitis ; sticking in side of neck ; numbness of arm ; twitching in forearm ; tension in thigh ; pressive pain in calf. Right : pressive pain in temple ; pimple in corner of mouth ; twitching in intercostal muscles ; rolling and gurgling in scapula ; pressive pain in fingers ; jerking pain in calf ; pressure in dorsum of foot ; stitching pains in sole. As if brain were constricted ; as if teeth were set on edge ; as if larynx were compressed ; as if closed, throat and larynx ; as if bubbles were forming and bursting in abdomen ; as if rolling and gurgling in right scapula ; as if limbs were bound or powerless. Pain : in region of spleen ; in region of liver. Jerking pain : in right calf. Needlelike stitches : in left temple. Stitches : in left forehead ; in abdomen, especially sides ; in chest ; in thighs, calves and soles ; in right sole. Pressing stitches : along spine ; in sacrum. Shooting : from epididymis over pubic region. Sticking : in left eyeball ; as with blunt needle on left side of nape. Stinging : in skin. Tearing : in occiput ; from ear to neck ; in lower limbs. Boring digging : in chest. Drawing pain : in left temple ; in external ear ; on dorsum of right foot. Burning : in left eyeball ; in mouth and fauces ; in throat ; in stomach ; in knees, legs and toes. Burning pressure : in sternum, < with exhalation. Biting : in skin with sweat. Smarting : in mouth and fauces. Soreness : in region of liver. Rawness : in mouth and fauces ; from mouth to stomach. Pressure : in occiput ; from region of liver into chest ; in chest ; on xiphoid process ; in fingers ; upon back of foot. Aching : across clavicles ; up left neck into ear. Tensive pain : in region of diaphragm with deep inhalation. Pressive pain : in right temple ; in left calf. Tension : in left thigh when stooping. Dulness : of head. Heaviness : in occiput. Numbness : in left arm. Itching : in perineum. Gastric bilious attacks, with mapped tongue. Neuralgia and rheumatism during and after typhus. TOUCH. PASSIVE MOTION. INJURIES. [45] [Tarax.]. Touch : > in right calf ; limbs painful. Boy, æt. 8 ; ague. Girl, æt. 19, servant ; typhoid fever. Man, æt. 46 ; ague. A man : unhealthy, pimply, sycotic skin.

keynotes.text: The young leaves of the Dandelion are sometimes eaten in salads. They have a bitter chicory-like taste, as also has the root. Children eat the flowers, although they are supposed by them to cause bed-wetting. This has given rise to one of the popular names of the plant. Its chief traditional uses are as a remedy for jaundice, liver obstruction, in dysentery, involuntary emissions, and in difficulties in passing water. Hahnemann's provings brought out these leading symptoms: "Painless urging to urinate;" and "frequent urging to urinate with copious discharge of urine." Hahnemann points out that this, with the thirst, may indicate Trx. in some cases of diabetes. Trx. has caused pains in both splenic and, liver regions, and has cured jaundice with enlarged indurated liver. Cooper gives me the following case: An old Indian officer, when in India, suffered much from gall-stones, and was advised to take dandelion infusion every day. This he did, and soon the symptoms left him, and he remained free from them for over twenty years. A symptom in connection with disordered digestion, which has proved a good guide for Trx. in some cases, is the "mapped tongue." The tongue is coated white, and clears off in patches, leaving dark red very sensitive spots. Treas. of Bot. points out that "the bright-yellow flowers of Trx. open in the morning between 5 and 6 o'clock and close in the evening between 8 and 9. Hence this was one of the plants selected by Linnæus to form his floral clock." The symptoms of Trx. are at night. Hands and nose cold at 8 p.m. "Cold finger-tips" is a guiding symptom. Peculiar Sensations are: As if brain was constricted. As if teeth were set on edge. As if larynx were compressed. As if bubbles were forming and bursting in abdomen. As of rolling and gurgling in right scapula. As if limbs were bound or powerless. Some leading indications are: Gastric and bilious attacks, especially gastric headaches; the headaches are generally pressive. Pressure in lower occiput after lying down is characteristic. Debility, loss of appetite, profuse night-sweats, especially when convalescing from bilious or typhoid fever. Restlessness of limbs in typhoid. Sweat between toes. The symptoms are: > By touch (jerking in right calf). Rest . (Almost all symptoms appear when sitting; lying down; resting.) Motion >. > In open air. Burning in throat is > by drinking. Hahnemann's dosage was a single drop of the Ø tincture (M. M. P.).

A Text-Book of Materia Medica. (Characteristic, Analytical, and Comparative.) By Pr. Allen Corson Cowperthwaite, M. D., PH. D., LL. D. TARAXACUM. (Dens Leonis.) Natural order.- Compositae. Common name.- Dandelion. General Analysis.- Acts especially upon the mucous membranes of the digestive tract, and upon the liver. Characteristic symptoms. Head.- (Taraxacum) Drawing pain in left temple while sitting, ceasing when walking and standing. Needle-like stitches in left temple when sitting, ceasing when standing. Tearing pain in occiput. Pressure and heaviness in lower part of occiput, after lying down. Eyes.- (Taraxacum) Burning in left eyeball. Ears.- (Taraxacum) Drawing pain in external ear. Face.- (Taraxacum) Hot and red. Mouth.- (Taraxacum) Teeth set on edge, as from acids. Tongue covered with a white coating, which peels of in patches, leaving dark, red, tender, very sensitive spots. Accumulation of saliva in the mouth, with sensation as if the larynx were pressed shut. Bitter taste in the mouth after eating. ( Bry., Nux v .) Stomach.- (Taraxacum) Bitter eructations ; hiccough. Nausea, as if from too fat food, with inclination to vomit. ( Puls .) Abdomen.- (Taraxacum) Stitching pains in sides (left) of abdomen. Motions in the abdomen, as if bubbles were forming and bursting. Stool.- (Taraxacum) Difficult, but not hard stool. Respiratory Organs.- (Taraxacum) Stitches in the chest. (Bry.) indications.text: Physiological Action—This agent acts mildly upon the liver as a cholagogue, and in consequence its laxative influence is mild. It stimulates the flow of bile into the duodenum, and encourages the eliminative changes carried on by the liver. It encourages the proper elaboration and elimination of urea, and the excretion of uric acid.

Therapy—It is valuable in combination with other remedies of similar action, in chronic jaundice, in conditions attributable to auto-intoxication, in rheumatism and in blood disorders, as an alterative. It is especially an alterative for chronic eruptions, and unhealthy conditions of the skin.
It will stimulate the stomach, and is useful in chronic catarrhal gastritis with perversion of nutrition. In aphthous ulcerations of the mouth it is useful.

The root of Taraxacum officinale, Weber, gathered in the autumn (Nat. Ord. Compositae). Native of Greece and a wayside weed in Europe and the United States. Dose, 5 to 60 grains.Common Name: Dandelion.

Principal Constituents.—Inulin, sugar, laevulin, and an amorphous, bitter taraxacin.Preparation.—Specific Medicine Taraxacum. Dose, 1 to 60 drops.Specific Indications.—Anorexia, weak digestion, hepatic torpor, and constipation.

Action and Therapy.—If prepared from recent root, taraxacum preparations may be classed with the simple bitters, having in addition a slight laxative, diuretic and alterative action. In association with other indicated remedies they may be used in catarrhal jaundice, with hepatic torpor, chronic constipation, and in catarrhal gastritis; also as a laxative-alterative in autointoxications giving rise to skin disorders and aphthous ulcers. It is contraindicated in weak and irritable or inflammatory conditions of the stomach and bowels, causing flatulence, pain, indigestion, and diarrhea. The best preparation is an extract of the fresh root.
The Eclectic Materia Medica, Pharmacology and Therapeutics, 1922, was written by Harvey Wickes Felter, M.D.

"The root of Taraxacum officinale, Weber, gathered in autumn"—(U. S. P.) (Leontodon taraxacum, Linné; Taraxacum Dens-leonis, Desfontaines; Taraxacum vulgare, Schrank). Nat. Ord.—Compositae. COMMON NAMES AND SYNONYM: Dandelion, Dandelion root; Taraxaci radix. ILLUSTRATION: Bentley and Trimen, Med. Plants, 159.

Botanical Source.—This plant is an herb with a perennial, tap-shaped, very milky root, with a dull-brownish epidermis. The leaves are radical, numerous, spreading, of a bright, shining green, quite smooth, tapering downward, sessile, pinnatifid, with runcinate, sharp, unequally toothed lobes. The scape or flower-stem is longer than the leaves, erect, round, smooth, brittle, naked, hollow, 5 or 6 inches in height, and bears a single, yellow head. The flowers are of a uniform golden-yellow, in round heads, 1 ½ inches in diameter, expanding in the morning and fine weather only. Involucre double; external scales small, closely pressed, and spreading or reflexed; internal ones in one row, erect, and linear; all frequently callous-horned at the apex. Florets numerous, strap-shaped, equal, and 5-toothed. Stamens with hair-like filaments. Receptacle naked, convex, and dotted. Ovary obovate; style slender and cylindrical; stigmas 2, revolute. Achenia oblong, ribbed, roughened on the ribs, apex prolonged into a very slender, thread-like beak, bearing the pappus of copious, soft, and white capillary bristles. After blossoming the inner involucre closes for a time, the slender beak elongates and raises up the pappus while the fruit is forming, the whole involucre is then reflexed, exposing to the wind the naked fruits, with the pappus displayed in an open globular head, nearly 2 inches in diameter (L.—G.—W.).

History and Description.—This plant is a native of Greece, but is now found growing abundantly in Europe and this country, in fields, gardens, and along roadsides, flowering from April to November. There are some other species recognized by botanists, that appear to possess the same medicinal powers. The young leaves are frequently used as a salad or greens. The whole plant, when broken or wounded, exudes a white, bitter juice, the sensible qualities of which are said to be greater during period of inflorescence. The root only is the official part, and should be collected while the plant is in flower, or preferably in autumn. When recent, it is from 3 to 5 inches or more in length, from 3 to 9 lines in diameter, tap-shaped, fleshy, dull-yellow or brownish externally, white internally, inodorous, and bitter. As found dried in pharmacy, it is considerably diminished in size, having lost more than half its weight, and corrugated lengthwise. As required by the U. S. P., it is slightly conical, about 30 Cm. (12 inches) long, and 1 or 2 Cm. (⅖ to ⅘ inch) thick above, crowned with several short, thickish heads, somewhat branched, dark-brown, longitudinally wrinkled, when dry breaking with a short fracture, showing a yellowish, porous central axis, surrounded by a thick, white bark, containing numerous milk-vessels, arranged in concentric circles; inodorous, bitter. It should be free from the root of Cichorium Intybus, Linné (Nat. Ord.—Compositae), which closely resembles it, but is usually paler, and has the milk-vessels in radiating lines"—(U. S. P.). Dandelion root should preferably be used in the recent state. Drying, as well as long boiling, impairs its virtues. Alcohol or boiling water extracts its properties.

Chemical Composition.—Taraxacum root contains inulin, the quantities of which vary considerably with the season. Dragendorff (1870) found 24 per cent in root collected in October, and only 1.74 per cent in root collected in March, shortly before blooming, at which season the milky juice is abundant. The root also contains variable quantities of reducing sugar (17 per cent in March), and laevulin (18.7 per cent). (See constituents at various seasons, by L. E. Savre, in Proc. Amer. Pharm. Assoc., 1893, p. 82; also paper, by Charles Symes, in Pharm. Jour. Trans., Vol. X, 1872, p. 361.) Other constituents of taraxacum root are a resin, soluble in chloroform and ether, insoluble in alcohol; a resin soluble in alcohol; taraxacerin of Kromayer (1861), a white waxy substance, crystallizing in cauliflower-like formations, and taraxacin (Polex, 1839), a bitter, amorphous principle, which, in concentrated solutions, forms precipitates with a number of alkaloidal reagents. Kromayer obtained these two principles from the inspissated milky juice of the root (leontodonium) by treating it with hot water. This leaves taraxacerin undissolved, which may then be obtained pure by recrystallization from hot alcohol. Prof. Sayre (Proc. Amer. Pharm. Assoc., 1897, p. 223) assigns to it the formula C9H15O. The aqueous extract of leontodonium contains the bitter principle; this is abstracted by charcoal, and the latter boiled out with alcohol. The latter is distilled off, the residue dissolved in water, precipitated by means of basic lead acetate, the lead removed from the filtrate by hydrogen sulphide, and the solution evaporated to dryness, and an acrid resin removed by ether. The residue is bitter taraxacin. (For a bibliography of taraxacum, see L. E. Sayre, Proc. Amer. Pharm. Assoc., 1876, p. 16-5.) A. van Zwaluwenburg and M. Gomberg (Proc. Amer. Pharm. Assoc., 1899, p. 305) also observed the presence of minute quantities of a substance giving alkaloidal reactions; however, the authors doubt its alkaloidal nature. Other constituents of taraxacum root, at least of partly fermented extracts, are calcium lactate and mannite. The leaves and stems, according to Marmé (1864), contain the sugar inosite. The incinerated root leaves 5 to 7 percent of ash.

Action, Medical Uses, and Dosage.—Dandelion root, when dried, possesses but little medicinal virtue; when recent, it is a stomachic and tonic, with slightly diuretic and aperient actions. It has long been supposed to exert an influence upon the biliary organs, removing torpor and engorgement of the liver as well as of the spleen. It is also reputed beneficial in dropsies, owing to want of action of the abdominal organs, in uterine obstructions, chronic diseases of the skin, and impairment of the digestive functions. It should not be used by those whose digestive organs are weak, as it is apt to occasion dyspepsia, flatulence, pain, and diarrhoea. The addition of cream of tartar to its decoction will render it more diuretic and laxative. Prof. King states that, as far as his experience with this article had gone, he thought its virtues had been overrated. Nevertheless, it is a slow, but efficient agent when properly prepared for use. The existence of an irritable condition of the stomach or bowels, or acute inflammation, contraindicate its employment. Dose of the decoction, 1 to 2 fluid ounces; of the extract, from 5 to 30 grains; of a strong tincture of the fresh root (℥viii to alcohol, 76 per cent, Oj), from 1 to 30 drops.
Specific Indications and Uses.—Loss of appetite, weak digestion, hepatic torpor, and constipation.
King's American Dispensatory, 1898, was written by Harvey Wickes Felter, M.D., and John Uri Lloyd, Phr. M., Ph. D.
